examples.title: Pseudocode: CUDA Kernel Result Pattern
examples.description: Demonstrates the fundamental pattern of writing results to device memory instead of returning values
examples.code:
ALGORITHM BasicKernelResultPattern
INPUT: n (number of elements), input_data array
OUTPUT: result array stored in device memory

// Step 1: Allocate device memory
BEGIN
 d_input = ALLOCATE_DEVICE_MEMORY(n * sizeof(float))
 d_result = ALLOCATE_DEVICE_MEMORY(n * sizeof(float))
 
 // Step 2: Copy input from host to device
 COPY_HOST_TO_DEVICE(d_input, input_data, n * sizeof(float))
 
 // Step 3: Launch kernel - threads write results to device memory
 threadsPerBlock = 256
 numBlocks = CEILING(n / threadsPerBlock)
 LAUNCH_KERNEL(vectorAdd, numBlocks, threadsPerBlock, d_input, d_result, n)
 
 // Step 4: Copy results back from device to host
 COPY_DEVICE_TO_HOST(result, d_result, n * sizeof(float))
 
 // Step 5: Free device memory
 FREE_DEVICE_MEMORY(d_input)
 FREE_DEVICE_MEMORY(d_result)
 
 RETURN result
END

ALGORITHM VectorAdd (CUDA Kernel)
INPUT: A, B (input arrays), C (output array), n (size)
OUTPUT: None (writes to device memory)

BEGIN
 // Calculate global thread index
 i = threadIdx.x + blockIdx.x * blockDim.x
 
 IF i < n THEN
 C[i] = A[i] + B[i] // Write result to device memory location
 ENDIF
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows the fundamental CUDA pattern: instead of using return statements, kernel threads write their computed results directly to device memory (arrays A, B, C). The host program allocates device memory, launches the kernel, then copies results back using cudaMemcpy with cudaMemcpyDeviceToHost direction.

examples.title: CUDA C: Vector Addition with Device Memory
examples.description: Complete working example showing the three-step pattern: allocate device memory, kernel writes results, copy back to host
examples.explanation: This complete CUDA program demonstrates the three-step result retrieval pattern: (1) cudaMalloc allocates device memory for input and output arrays, (2) the vectorAdd kernel runs thousands of threads in parallel, each writing its result to a unique index in the output array C, and (3) cudaMemcpy with cudaMemcpyDeviceToHost copies the results back to the host. The CUDA_CHECK macro provides error handling for both memory operations and kernel launches.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) - each of n elements processed once
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) - device arrays same size as input

examples.title: CUDA C: Parallel Reduction for Scalar Results
examples.description: Efficient tree-based reduction in shared memory to compute a single sum from thousands of threads
examples.explanation: This example implements parallel reduction, the standard technique for computing a scalar result (sum) from thousands of parallel threads. The key insight is using shared memory for fast in-block reduction (O(log n) steps with __syncthreads synchronization), then having thread 0 of each block write its partial sum to global memory. Block results are then combined either on the host or with a second kernel. This avoids the race condition that would occur if all threads tried to write to the same memory location simultaneously.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n/blockSize * log(blockSize)) - each block reduces in O(log blockSize)
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) + O(numBlocks) - input array plus block sums array

examples.title: CUDA C: Atomic Operations for Simple Accumulation
examples.description: Using atomicAdd for thread-safe accumulation when reduction overhead is not justified
examples.explanation: This example shows two approaches using atomicAdd: (1) direct atomicAdd from every thread - simple but creates a severe bottleneck as thousands of threads compete for the same memory location, and (2) block-level atomicAdd where each block first reduces to a partial sum in shared memory (fast, no contention), then only thread 0 of each block calls atomicAdd once. The second approach dramatically reduces atomic operations from n to numBlocks. Key point: always initialize result variables to 0 before using atomicAdd, otherwise you get garbage values.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) with high constant factor due to serialization
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for input plus O(1) for result
